# %cd "/content/drive/My Drive/Assignment2"

CSE 258 A2: Australian Steam Bundles¶

Overview¶

Bundles play a significant role in the Steam ecosystem, with approximately one-third of users purchasing at least one bundle, and bundled games accounting for roughly 70% of all game acquisitions (Pathak et al., 2017). This motivated our investigation into personalized bundle recommendation systems that can better capture user preferences and bundle characteristics.

We propose a solution to Steam bundle recommendation. We select datasets from the Steam video game distribution network, retrieved from https://cseweb.ucsd.edu/~jmcauley/datasets.html#steam_data.

In this notebook, we will go over the following sections:

  • Exploratory Analysis on the Steam datasets

  • Task Desciption, which outlines the objectives and methodology of this project

  • Data Processing to formulate various data organizations

  • Prediction Models, 4 approaches

  • Results and Evaluation

  • Related Work

  • Conclusion

Exploratory Analysis¶

The data dumps provide 5 datasets:

  • australian_users_items contains library information for each user. Each record holds the user id, their url, number of games, and list of game ids, names, and playtimes. The distribution of number of purchased games has a very long right tail, while most users own 0-10 games.

  • australian_user_reviews contains reviews per users. Each record holds the user id, their url, and list of their reviews. This dataset overlaps with australian_users_items and is not compatible.

  • bundle_data contains essential data for all bundles available in this project: id, name, url, price, discount, and notably list of items. It also has a long right tail whereas most bundles have only 2 games.

  • steam_games contains detailed data for games appeared in bundles and beyond: id, name, url, developer, publisher, genres, tags, release date, specs, price, and early_access.

  • steam_reviews contains a large number of reviews. Each record holds the username, their hours, their number of games, the game name, page, date, text, and early_access.

Although reviews encompass abundant hints on what kind of games a user prefer, we opt to focus on more features pertinent to the games. As a result, we will be using 3 datasets among all:

  • australian_users_items

  • bundle_data

  • steam_games

Please refer to the code outputs for more insights.

In [ ]:
import ast
from collections import defaultdict
import gzip
import numpy as np
import os
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import random
In [ ]:
def extract_gz(path, extraction_rate=1.00):
    with gzip.open(path, 'rt', encoding='utf-8') as f:
        n = sum(1 for _ in f)
        print(f'    Total lines: {n}')
        print(f'    Extracting {int(extraction_rate * 100)}% of lines: {int(n * extraction_rate)}')
        f.seek(0)
        dropped = set()
        if extraction_rate < 1.00:
            dropped = set(random.sample(range(1, n + 1), int(n * (1 - extraction_rate))))
        i = 0
        for line in f:
            i += 1
            if i not in dropped:
                l = line.strip()
                if l:
                    yield ast.literal_eval(l)
            if i % 1000000 == 0:
                print(f'    Processed {i} lines...')
In [ ]:
# Load datasets
project_path = "."
dataset_path = os.path.join(project_path, "datasets")
datasets = defaultdict(list)
extraction_rates = {
    'australian_users_items.json.gz': 1.00,
    'australian_user_reviews.json.gz': 1.00,
    'bundle_data.json.gz': 1.00,
    'steam_games.json.gz': 1.00,
    'steam_reviews.json.gz': 1.00  # Cut down this (steam_reviews) if needs to reduce size
}

for filename in os.listdir(dataset_path):
    if not filename.endswith('.gz'):
        continue
    filepath = os.path.join(dataset_path, filename)
    if not os.path.isfile(filepath):
        continue
    print(f'Loading dataset from {filepath}')
    data = []
    for d in extract_gz(filepath, extraction_rate=extraction_rates.get(filename, 1.00)):
        data.append(d)
    datasets[filename.split('.')[0]] = data
print('All datasets loaded')

In [ ]:
# Display information of records per loaded dataset
sizes = {name: len(data) for name, data in datasets.items()}
if not sizes:
    print('No datasets loaded.')
else:
    sizes_series = pd.Series(sizes)
    print('Dataset sizes (number of records):')
    print(sizes_series.to_string())

    # Print a preview (first record) for each dataset
    for name in sizes_series.index:
        count = sizes[name]
        print(f'\n-- {name} - {count} records')
        if count > 0:
            # Show an untruncated representation of the first record
            preview = repr(datasets[name][0])
            print('   first record preview:', preview)
            print('   first record keys:', list(datasets[name][0].keys()))

-- australian_user_reviews - 25799 records
   first record preview: {'user_id': '76561197970982479', 'user_url': 'http://steamcommunity.com/profiles/76561197970982479', 'reviews': [{'funny': '', 'posted': 'Posted November 5, 2011.', 'last_edited': '', 'item_id': '1250', 'helpful': 'No ratings yet', 'recommend': True, 'review': 'Simple yet with great replayability. In my opinion does "zombie" hordes and team work better than left 4 dead plus has a global leveling system. Alot of down to earth "zombie" splattering fun for the whole family. Amazed this sort of FPS is so rare.'}, {'funny': '', 'posted': 'Posted July 15, 2011.', 'last_edited': '', 'item_id': '22200', 'helpful': 'No ratings yet', 'recommend': True, 'review': "It's unique and worth a playthrough."}, {'funny': '', 'posted': 'Posted April 21, 2011.', 'last_edited': '', 'item_id': '43110', 'helpful': 'No ratings yet', 'recommend': True, 'review': 'Great atmosphere. The gunplay can be a bit chunky at times but at the end of the day this game is definitely worth it and I hope they do a sequel...so buy the game so I get a sequel!'}]}
   first record keys: ['user_id', 'user_url', 'reviews']

   first record preview: {'username': 'Chaos Syren', 'hours': 0.1, 'products': 41, 'product_id': '725280', 'page_order': 0, 'date': '2017-12-17', 'text': 'This would not be acceptable as an entertainment even back in the day when these graphics were all there was to be had. # Compute items count distribution for australian_user_items
aui = datasets.get('australian_users_items', [])
if not aui:
    print('`australian_users_items` is not loaded.')
else:
    print('Computing items count distribution for `australian_users_items`')
    items_counts = []
    for r in aui:
        if isinstance(r, dict):
            # Prefer an explicit items_count field when available
            if 'items_count' in r and isinstance(r['items_count'], (int, float)):
                items_counts.append(int(r['items_count']))
            elif 'items' in r and isinstance(r['items'], (list, tuple)):
                items_counts.append(len(r['items']))
    if not items_counts:
        print('No items count values available in records.')
    else:
        min_count = int(min(items_counts))
        max_count = int(max(items_counts))
        print(f'Min items count: {min_count}')
        print(f'Max items count: {max_count}')
        bins = np.linspace(0, 500, 50)
        plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
        plt.hist(items_counts, bins=bins, edgecolor='black')
        plt.xlabel('Items count')
        plt.ylabel('Users')
        plt.title('Number of users per items count')
        plt.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.5)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

Computing items count distribution for `australian_users_items`
Min items count: 0
Max items count: 7762
In [ ]:
# Compute items count distribution for australian_user_items
bd = datasets.get('bundle_data', [])
if not bd:
    print('`bundle_data` is not loaded.')
else:
    print('Computing items count distribution for `bundle_data`')
    items_counts = []
    for r in bd:
        if isinstance(r, dict):
            # Prefer an explicit items_count field when available
            if 'items_count' in r and isinstance(r['items_count'], (int, float)):
                items_counts.append(int(r['items_count']))
            elif 'items' in r and isinstance(r['items'], (list, tuple)):
                items_counts.append(len(r['items']))
    if not items_counts:
        print('No items count values available in records.')
    else:
        min_count = int(min(items_counts))
        max_count = int(max(items_counts))
        print(f'Min items count: {min_count}')
        print(f'Max items count: {max_count}')
        bins = np.linspace(0, 100, 50)
        plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
        plt.hist(items_counts, bins=bins, color='red', edgecolor='black')
        plt.xlabel('Items count')
        plt.ylabel('Users')
        plt.title('Number of bundles per items count')
        plt.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.5)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

Computing items count distribution for `bundle_data`
Min items count: 2
Max items count: 89

Task Description¶

1. Predictive Task¶

The objective of this project is to build a personalized bundle recommendation system for Steam users. Given a user’s historical game library, playtime behavior, and the catalog of available game bundles, the predictive task is:

For a given user u, estimate the probability that they will be interested in (or likely to purchase) a bundle b.

Formally, we aim to learn: $$\hat{y}_{u,b} = f(u, b),$$ where $\hat{y}_{u,b}$ represents the predicted affinity score of user $u$ for bundle $b$.

Why This Task Matters¶

Steam bundles provide users with discounted collections of games. Accurate bundle recommendations:

  • increase Steam’s revenue by promoting bundles users are likely to purchase,
  • help users find relevant bundles at lower prices,
  • improve overall user satisfaction and discovery.

This task aligns well with course content on recommendation systems, ranking models, and evaluation of predictors using implicit feedback.

2. Baseline and Model Overview¶

  • Logistic Regression Baseline

User–bundle interactions are represented using one-hot and numerical features (e.g., bundle popularity, item overlap, user activity). A logistic regression classifier is trained to predict whether a user would choose a bundle. This provides a simple linear baseline.

  • Bayesian Personalized Ranking (BPR)

A collaborative-filtering model that learns user and bundle representations by optimizing pairwise ranking loss. BPR directly models user preferences by ensuring that observed (positive) bundles are ranked higher than unobserved ones. This provides a personalized recommendation baseline grounded in latent factor modeling.

  • Latent Facotr Model

Users and bundles are embedded into a shared latent space. Their interaction score is computed via dot product. This captures hidden preference patterns with minimal complexity.

  • Neural Collaborative Filtering

A more expressive collaborative model that uses nonlinear layers to learn user–bundle interactions. It provides a deeper baseline that bridges traditional MF and neural models

3. Assessing the Validity of Model Predictions¶

We will evaluate the validity of our recommendation models using standard metrics for implicit-feedback ranking tasks:

  • AUC (Area Under the ROC Curve): Measures how well the model ranks positive user–bundle interactions above negative ones. Higher AUC indicates better pairwise ranking performance.

  • Top-K Recommendation Metrics: We will compute Recall@10 and Recall@50, to assess how effectively the model retrieves relevant bundles within the top-K recommendations. These metrics reflect actual recommendation quality from a user perspective.

  • Cross-Validation on Held-Out Users: Validation splits ensure that hyperparameters and model choices generalize rather than overfit. Final performance is reported on a separate test set using the same metrics.

Together, these evaluations provide a robust assessment of both ranking accuracy and practical top-K recommendation quality.

Data Processing¶

Steam Bundle Preprocessing Pipeline¶

The following code is the full preprocessing pipeline used to transform raw Steam datasets into structured user–bundle, user–item, and bundle–item mappings. It consolidates item metadata, filters invalid entries, generates training/test splits, and saves all processed data as JSON files.

1. Input Datasets¶

The script loads three raw datasets:

Dataset Roles¶

  • australian_users_items — list of users and the items they own.
  • bundle_data — each bundle contains a list of item IDs.
  • steam_games — full metadata for each game.
In [ ]:
aus_items   = datasets['australian_users_items']
bundle_raw  = datasets['bundle_data']
games_raw   = datasets['steam_games']

2. Collect All Unique Item IDs¶

Item IDs are collected from:

  • user item lists
  • bundle item lists

A helper function parses bundle item ID strings, ensuring all valid numeric IDs are included.

In [ ]:
item_ids = set()
for u in aus_items:
    for it in u.get("items", []):
        item_ids.add(str(it["item_id"]))

def parse_bundle_item_ids(raw_id):
    if raw_id is None:
        return []
    s = str(raw_id).strip()
    if not s:
        return []

    appids = []
    for part in s.split(','):
        p = part.strip()
        if p.isdigit():
            appids.append(p)
    return appids

for b in bundle_raw:
    for it in b.get("items", []):
        for appid in parse_bundle_item_ids(it.get("item_id")):
            item_ids.add(appid)

game_by_id = {}
for g in games_raw:
    if "id" in g:
        item_ids.add(str(g["id"]))
        game_by_id[str(g["id"])] = g

3. Build Game Metadata Table¶

For each item ID:

  • Extract metadata fields: title, genres, tags, price, release_date, etc.
  • Handle missing fields with defaults.
  • Create lookup tables:
    • item_id_lookup[appid] = index
    • appid2idx[index] = appid

Items are sorted by integer appid.

In [ ]:
sorted_ids = sorted(item_ids, key=lambda x: int(x))
item_id_lookup = {idx: int(appid) for idx, appid in enumerate(sorted_ids)}
appid2idx = {appid: idx for idx, appid in enumerate(sorted_ids)}
items_set = set(range(len(sorted_ids)))
item_data = []
for appid in sorted_ids:
    g = game_by_id.get(appid)
    if g is None:
        info = {
            "appid": appid,
            "title": None,
            "genres": [],
            "tags": [],
            "price": None,
            "discount_price": None,
            "release_date": None,
            "early_access": None,
            "developer": None,
            "publisher": None,
        }
    else:
        info = {
            "appid": appid,
            "title": g.get("title") or g.get("app_name"),
            "genres": g.get("genres", []),
            "tags": g.get("tags", []),
            "price": g.get("price"),
            "discount_price": g.get("discount_price"),
            "release_date": g.get("release_date"),
            "early_access": g.get("early_access"),
            "developer": g.get("developer"),
            "publisher": g.get("publisher"),
        }
    item_data.append(info)

4. Build Bundle → Items Mapping¶

For each bundle:

bundle_item_map[bundle_idx] = {item_idx1, item_idx2, ...}

Only valid items are kept.

Additional lookup tables:

  • bundle_id2idx
  • idx2bundle_id
In [ ]:
bundle_item_map = defaultdict(set)
bundle_item_map_bpr = defaultdict(set)
bundle_id2idx = {}
idx2bundle_id = {}

for b_idx, b in enumerate(bundle_raw):
    bundle_id = str(b["bundle_id"])
    bundle_id2idx[bundle_id] = b_idx
    idx2bundle_id[b_idx] = bundle_id

    bundle_item_map[b_idx] = set()  # <-- ensure key exists even if empty

    for it in b.get("items", []):
        appid = str(it["item_id"])
        if appid in appid2idx:
            item_idx = appid2idx[appid]
            bundle_item_map[b_idx].add(item_idx)
            bundle_item_map_bpr[b_idx].add(item_idx)  # for BPR model

5. Build User → Items Mapping¶

For each user:

user_item_map[user_idx] = {item_idx1, item_idx2, ...}

Only valid items are added.

Mappings:

  • user_id2idx
  • idx2user_id
In [ ]:
user_item_map = defaultdict(set)
user_item_map_bpr = defaultdict(set)
user_id2idx = {}
idx2user_id = {}

for u_idx, u in enumerate(aus_items):
    uid = str(u["user_id"])
    user_id2idx[uid] = u_idx
    idx2user_id[u_idx] = uid

    user_item_map[u_idx] = set()

    for it in u.get("items", []):
        appid = str(it["item_id"])
        if appid in appid2idx:
            item_idx = appid2idx[appid]
            user_item_map[u_idx].add(item_idx)
            user_item_map_bpr[u_idx].add(item_idx)  # for BPR model

6. Filter Invalid Users & Bundles¶

Keep only:

  • users with ≥ 1 owned item
  • bundles containing ≥ 1 valid item

This produces:

  • filtered_users
  • filtered_bundles

New compact ID mappings are created.

7. Rebuild Maps with New IDs¶

Maps reconstructed:

  • new_user_item_map
  • new_bundle_item_map

Using only filtered entities and new indices.

In [ ]:
filtered_users = []
for u_idx, items in user_item_map.items():
    if len(items) > 0:
        filtered_users.append(u_idx)

filtered_bundles = []
for b_idx, items in bundle_item_map.items():
    if len(items) > 0:
        filtered_bundles.append(b_idx)

new_user_id = {old_u: new_u for new_u, old_u in enumerate(filtered_users)}
new_bundle_id = {old_b: new_b for new_b, old_b in enumerate(filtered_bundles)}

num_users   = len(filtered_users)
num_bundles = len(filtered_bundles)
num_items   = len(appid2idx)

new_user_item_map = defaultdict(set)
for old_u in filtered_users:
    new_u = new_user_id[old_u]
    new_user_item_map[new_u] = set(user_item_map[old_u])

new_bundle_item_map = defaultdict(set)
for old_b in filtered_bundles:
    new_b = new_bundle_id[old_b]
    new_bundle_item_map[new_b] = set(bundle_item_map[old_b])

bundle_items_list = new_bundle_item_map

print(num_users)
print(num_bundles)
print(num_items)

71504
613
34239

8. Build User → Bundle Ownership Mapping¶

A user owns a bundle if all bundle items are a subset of the user’s owned items:

if b_items.issubset(items):
    user_bundle_map[u].add(b)

This rule defines the ground-truth user-bundle interactions

In [ ]:
user_bundle_map_bpr = defaultdict(set)
bundle_items_list_bpr = list(bundle_item_map_bpr.items())
for u_idx, items_owned in user_item_map_bpr.items():
    if not items_owned:
        continue
    for b_idx, b_items in bundle_items_list_bpr:
        if b_items and b_items.issubset(items_owned):
            user_bundle_map_bpr[u_idx].add(b_idx)

user_bundle_map = defaultdict(set)
for u, items in new_user_item_map.items():
    for b, b_items in new_bundle_item_map.items():
        if b_items.issubset(items):  # user owns all games in bundle
            user_bundle_map[u].add(b)

9. Construct Training/Test set¶

Split 80/20 Training/Test set

Prevent data leakage¶

Build training-only map:

  • user_bundle_map_train
In [ ]:
item_data_map=dict()
tags_set=set()
for item in item_data:
    item_data_map[int(item['appid'])]=item
    for tag in item['tags']:
        tags_set.add(tag)
tags_map=dict()
for i,tag in enumerate(tags_set):
    tags_map[tag]=i
def get_feat(tags):
    feat=np.zeros(len(tags_map))
    for tag in tags:
        feat[tags_map[tag]]=1
    return feat

# BPR data preparation

all_data_bpr=[]

for user,bundles in user_bundle_map_bpr.items():
    for bundle in bundles:
        all_data_bpr.append((user,bundle))

all_item_data_bpr=[]
for user,items in user_item_map_bpr.items():
    for item in items:
        all_item_data_bpr.append((user,item))

random.shuffle(all_data_bpr)
data_size=len(all_data_bpr)

training_data_bpr=all_data_bpr[:int(0.8*data_size)]
test_data_bpr=all_data_bpr[int(0.8*data_size):]

user_bundle_map_train_bpr = defaultdict(set)
for user, bundle in training_data_bpr:
    user_bundle_map_train_bpr[user].add(bundle)

# Common data preparation

all_data=[]

for user,bundles in user_bundle_map.items():
    for bundle in bundles:
        all_data.append((user,bundle))

all_item_data=[]
for user,items in user_item_map.items():
    for item in items:
        all_item_data.append((user,item))

random.shuffle(all_data)
data_size=len(all_data)

training_data=all_data[:int(0.8*data_size)]
test_data=all_data[int(0.8*data_size):]

# Create training-only user_bundle_map to avoid data leakage
# This map only contains user-bundle pairs from training_data
user_bundle_map_train = defaultdict(set)
# user_item_map_train = defaultdict(set)

for user, bundle in training_data:
    user_bundle_map_train[user].add(bundle)
    # items_in_bundle = new_bundle_item_map[bundle]
    # user_item_map_train[user].update(items_in_bundle)

print(f"Total user-bundle pairs: {len(all_data)}")
print(f"Training pairs: {len(training_data)} ({len(training_data)/len(all_data)*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"Test pairs: {len(test_data)} ({len(test_data)/len(all_data)*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"Training users in map: {len(user_bundle_map_train)}")
print(f"Total users in full map: {len(user_bundle_map)}")

Total user-bundle pairs: 102520
Training pairs: 82016 (80.0%)
Test pairs: 20504 (20.0%)
Training users in map: 31554
Total users in full map: 34460

10. Save Processed Data as JSON¶

Saved to:

pgsql
./data/processed_json/

All structures serialized to JSON-friendly format.

Output Files¶

File Description
item_set.json list of all item IDs
item_id_lookup.json item_id → index
all_items.json cleaned metadata for each item
user_item_map.json user → items owned
user_bundle_map.json user → bundles owned
bundle_item_map.json bundle → items contained
In [ ]:
import os, json
SAVE_DIR = "./data/processed_json"
os.makedirs(SAVE_DIR, exist_ok=True)

def to_jsonable(obj):
    if isinstance(obj, set):
        return list(obj)
    if hasattr(obj, "tolist"):
        return obj.tolist()
    if isinstance(obj, dict):
        return {str(k): to_jsonable(v) for k, v in obj.items()}
    if isinstance(obj, (list, tuple)):
        return [to_jsonable(x) for x in obj]
    return obj

def save_json(obj, filename):
    filepath = os.path.join(SAVE_DIR, filename)
    with open(filepath, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        json.dump(to_jsonable(obj), f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)
    print(f"[saved] {filepath}")
In [ ]:
save_json(items_set, "item_set.json")
save_json(item_id_lookup, "item_id_lookup.json")
save_json(item_data, "all_items.json")
save_json(user_item_map, "user_item_map.json")
save_json(user_bundle_map, "user_bundle_map.json")
save_json(bundle_item_map, "bundle_item_map.json")

[saved] ./data/processed_json/item_set.json
[saved] ./data/processed_json/item_id_lookup.json
[saved] ./data/processed_json/all_items.json
[saved] ./data/processed_json/user_item_map.json
[saved] ./data/processed_json/user_bundle_map.json
[saved] ./data/processed_json/bundle_item_map.json

11. Graph Sampling¶

1. Purpose of the Sampling Procedure¶

graph_sampling generates user–positive–negative triplets , based on the user–bundle bipartite graph.

  • training_data contains only positive edges (user, bundle) where the user truly owns the bundle.
  • The function creates negative samples (user, negative_bundle) such that:
    • the user does not own the negative bundle,
    • the negative bundle is "reasonably hard" (i.e., not random noise),
    • the samples are balanced and consistent across users.

This function takes advantage of graph structure instead of uniformly random sample

2. check_tuple: Verifying a Valid Pair of Edges¶

For two positive edges:

tuple_1 = (u1, b1)
tuple_2 = (u2, b2)

The function:

tuple_1[1] not in user_bundle_map[tuple_2[0]]
and
tuple_2[1] not in user_bundle_map[tuple_1[0]]

means:

  • b1 is not owned by u2
  • b2 is not owned by u1

If both conditions hold, then:

  • (u1, b2) is a valid negative sample
  • (u2, b1) is also a valid negative sample

Thus, one valid pair of edges produces two symmetric training samples:

  1. (u1, pos=b1, neg=b2)
  2. (u2, pos=b2, neg=b1)

This makes sampling more efficient and yields harder negatives compared to global random sampling.

3. Inputs and Outputs¶

Inputs¶

Argument Meaning
n_samples Number of triplets to generate (must be even, function creates 2 per iteration)
training_data List of positive user–bundle pairs (user, bundle)
user_bundle_map Mapping user → set(bundles) for fast membership tests

Outputs¶

The function returns three lists:

  • sgd_users[k] — user ID
  • sgd_pos_items[k] — positive bundle
  • sgd_neg_items[k] — negative bundle

For each index k, (sgd_users[k], sgd_pos_items[k], sgd_neg_items[k]) forms a valid training triplet.

4. Why This is Called Graph-Based Sampling¶

The method works directly on the user–bundle bipartite graph:

  • Positive edges = observed interactions
  • Negative edges = inferred missing edges
  • Sampling considers graph structure, not random guessing
  • A valid pair of positive edges must satisfy structural constraints

This produces "hard negatives", superior to naive random selection.

5. Advantages Over Traditional Negative Sampling¶

Method Description Weakness
Global random sampling Pick a random bundle → check if user doesn’t own it Often produces trivial negatives
Uniform per-user sampling Random from bundles not owned Still mostly easy negatives; ignores graph structure
Graph sampling (this method) Uses cross-edge inference to generate hard negatives More computationally expensive

Graph sampling yields:

  • Harder negatives
  • Higher-quality gradients
  • Better ranking performance
  • Balanced sampling (every valid tuple generates 2 samples)

6. Potential Limitations¶

  1. Efficiency If the graph is dense, many random pairs might fail the check_tuple test.

  2. Sample imbalance Users with many interactions appear more often because sampling is edge-based.

  3. n_samples must be even Each iteration contributes exactly 2 samples.

  4. High variance Since sampling is random, results vary unless seed is fixed.

The following code is reproduced and edited from the official implementation of Pathak et al. [3]：

In [ ]:
def check_tuple(tuple_1, tuple_2, user_bundle_map):
    return tuple_1[1] not in user_bundle_map[tuple_2[0]] and tuple_2[1] not in user_bundle_map[tuple_1[0]]
In [ ]:
def graph_sampling(n_samples, training_data, user_bundle_map):
    sgd_users=[]
    sgd_pos_items, sgd_neg_items = [], []
    i=0
    while n_samples>0:
        if i%100000==0:
            print(i)
        i+=1
        tuple_1=training_data[np.random.randint(len(training_data))]
        tuple_2=training_data[np.random.randint(len(training_data))]
        iteration=100
        while not check_tuple(tuple_1, tuple_2, user_bundle_map):
            tuple_2=training_data[np.random.randint(len(training_data))]
            iteration-=1
            if iteration == 0:
                break
        if iteration==0:
            continue
        sgd_neg_items.append(tuple_2[1])
        sgd_pos_items.append(tuple_1[1])
        sgd_users.append(tuple_1[0])

        sgd_neg_items.append(tuple_1[1])
        sgd_pos_items.append(tuple_2[1])
        sgd_users.append(tuple_2[0])
        n_samples-=2
    return sgd_users, sgd_pos_items, sgd_neg_items
In [ ]:
sgd_users_train, sgd_pos_bundles_train, sgd_neg_bundles_train = graph_sampling(
    len(training_data)*30, training_data, user_bundle_map_train
)

0
100000
200000
300000
400000
500000
600000
700000
800000
900000
1000000
1100000
1200000
In [ ]:
def item_sampling(n_samples, all_item_data, user_item_map):
    sgd_users=[]
    sgd_pos_items, sgd_neg_items = [], []
    i=0
    while n_samples>0:
        if i%100000==0:
            print(i)
        i+=1
        tuple_1=all_item_data[np.random.randint(len(all_item_data))]
        tuple_2=all_item_data[np.random.randint(len(all_item_data))]
        iteration=100
        while not tuple_1[1] not in user_item_map[tuple_2[0]] and tuple_2[1] not in user_item_map[tuple_1[0]]:
            tuple_2=all_item_data[np.random.randint(len(all_item_data))]
            iteration-=1
            if iteration == 0:
                break
        if iteration==0:
            continue
        sgd_neg_items.append(tuple_2[1])
        sgd_pos_items.append(tuple_1[1])
        sgd_users.append(tuple_1[0])

        sgd_neg_items.append(tuple_1[1])
        sgd_pos_items.append(tuple_2[1])
        sgd_users.append(tuple_2[0])
        n_samples-=2
    return sgd_users, sgd_pos_items, sgd_neg_items
In [ ]:
item_sgd_users, sgd_pos_items, sgd_neg_items = item_sampling(len(all_item_data_bpr), all_item_data_bpr, user_item_map_bpr)

0
100000
200000
300000
400000
500000
600000
700000

1000000

12. Train/Validation/Test Triplet Generation¶

1. Training & Validation Triplets¶

Triplets are generated from training_data using training-only user–bundle map (user_bundle_map_train), which prevents information from test sets leaking into the sampling process.

Train/Val Split¶

We split the triplets (from the 80% train portion) into:

  • 70% train
  • 10% validation

2. Test Triplets (From Test Data)¶

Test triplets are generated from test_data only.

In [ ]:
# Generate train/val triples from training_data using training-only map
print("Generating train/val triples from training data...")

train_val_triples = list(zip(sgd_users_train, sgd_pos_bundles_train, sgd_neg_bundles_train))
random.shuffle(train_val_triples)

# 70-10 split for train-val (from training_data)
train_val_split = int(0.875 * len(train_val_triples))
train_triples = train_val_triples[:train_val_split]
val_triples   = train_val_triples[train_val_split:]

print("Generating test triples from held-out test data...")
sgd_users_test, sgd_pos_bundles_test, sgd_neg_bundles_test = graph_sampling(
    len(test_data)*30, test_data, user_bundle_map
)

test_triples = list(zip(sgd_users_test, sgd_pos_bundles_test, sgd_neg_bundles_test))
random.shuffle(test_triples)

train_users  = [t[0] for t in train_triples]
train_pos    = [t[1] for t in train_triples]
train_neg    = [t[2] for t in train_triples]

val_users    = [t[0] for t in val_triples]
val_pos      = [t[1] for t in val_triples]
val_neg      = [t[2] for t in val_triples]

test_users   = [t[0] for t in test_triples]
test_pos     = [t[1] for t in test_triples]
test_neg     = [t[2] for t in test_triples]

total_triples = len(train_triples) + len(val_triples) + len(test_triples)
print(f"\nSplit Summary:")
print(f"Train: {len(train_triples)} triples ({len(train_triples)/total_triples*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"Val: {len(val_triples)} triples ({len(val_triples)/total_triples*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"Test: {len(test_triples)} triples ({len(test_triples)/total_triples*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"\nIMPORTANT: Test triples are generated from held-out test_data to avoid data leakage!")

Generating train/val triples from training data...
Generating test triples from held-out test data...
0
100000
200000
300000

Split Summary:
Train: 2152920 triples (70.0%)
Val: 307560 triples (10.0%)
Test: 615120 triples (20.0%)

IMPORTANT: Test triples are generated from held-out test_data to avoid data leakage!

13. Data Leakage Check¶

To ensure a clean evaluation, we verify that test triplets do not overlap with training data.

In [ ]:
# Verify no data leakage: Check that test triples don't overlap with training data
print("="*60)
print("DATA LEAKAGE CHECK")
print("="*60)

# Convert training_data to set for fast lookup
training_data_set = set(training_data)
test_data_set = set(test_data)

# Check test triples
test_pos_pairs = set(zip(test_users, test_pos))
test_neg_pairs = set(zip(test_users, test_neg))

# Count overlaps
train_pos_overlap = len(test_pos_pairs & training_data_set)
train_neg_overlap = len(test_neg_pairs & training_data_set)

print(f"Test positive pairs that appear in training_data: {train_pos_overlap}")
print(f"Test negative pairs that appear in training_data: {train_neg_overlap}")
print(f"Total test positive pairs: {len(test_pos_pairs)}")
print(f"Total test negative pairs: {len(test_neg_pairs)}")

if train_pos_overlap == 0 and train_neg_overlap == 0:
    print("\n✓ NO DATA LEAKAGE DETECTED: Test set is properly isolated!")
else:
    print(f"\n⚠ WARNING: Found {train_pos_overlap + train_neg_overlap} overlapping pairs!")
    print("This indicates potential data leakage.")

# Check that test_data doesn't overlap with training_data
overlap = len(training_data_set & test_data_set)
print(f"\nOverlap between training_data and test_data: {overlap}")
if overlap == 0:
    print("✓ Training and test data are properly separated!")
else:
    print(f"⚠ WARNING: Found {overlap} overlapping user-bundle pairs!")

print("="*60)

============================================================
DATA LEAKAGE CHECK
============================================================
Test positive pairs that appear in training_data: 0
Test negative pairs that appear in training_data: 0
Total test positive pairs: 20504
Total test negative pairs: 163877

✓ NO DATA LEAKAGE DETECTED: Test set is properly isolated!

Overlap between training_data and test_data: 0
✓ Training and test data are properly separated!
============================================================

Prediction Model¶

(A) Baseline Model: Logistic Regression¶

1. Constructing Training and Test Pairs¶

We first turn the sampled triplets into a binary classification dataset.

  • Each pair (u, b) is a user–bundle pair.
  • Labels:
    • y = 1 if the bundle is sampled as the positive bundle for that user.
    • y = 0 if the bundle is a sampled negative bundle.
  • At the same time, we maintain:
    • bundle_pop_counter[b] — how many times bundle b appears as a positive sample (a proxy for popularity).
    • user_activity_counter[u] — how many positive interactions user u has (a proxy for user activity).

2. Popularity and Activity Features¶

We normalize bundle popularity and user activity to $[0, 1]$:

For bundle $b$ with positive count $c_b$:

$$ \text{pop}(b) = \frac{c_b}{\max_{b'} c_{b'}} \in [0, 1] $$

For user $u$ with positive count $c_u$:

$$ \text{act}(u) = \frac{c_u}{\max_{u'} c_{u'}} \in [0, 1] $$

These become two global scalar features for each user–bundle pair.

In [ ]:
from collections import Counter
X_train_pairs = []
y_train = []
X_test_pairs = []
y_test = []

bundle_pop_counter = Counter()
user_activity_counter = Counter()

for u, b_pos, b_neg in zip(sgd_users_train, sgd_pos_bundles_train, sgd_neg_bundles_train):
    X_train_pairs.append([u, b_pos])
    y_train.append(1)
    bundle_pop_counter[b_pos] += 1
    user_activity_counter[u] += 1
    X_train_pairs.append([u, b_neg])
    y_train.append(0)

for u, b_pos, b_neg in zip(sgd_users_test, sgd_pos_bundles_test, sgd_neg_bundles_test):
    X_test_pairs.append([u, b_pos])
    y_test.append(1)
    X_test_pairs.append([u, b_neg])
    y_test.append(0)

X_train_pairs = np.array(X_train_pairs)
y_train = np.array(y_train)
X_test_pairs = np.array(X_test_pairs)
y_test = np.array(y_test)

max_pop = max(bundle_pop_counter.values()) if bundle_pop_counter else 1.0
bundle_popularity = {int(b): float(c) / max_pop for b, c in bundle_pop_counter.items()}

max_act = max(user_activity_counter.values()) if user_activity_counter else 1
user_activity = {int(u): c / max_act for u, c in user_activity_counter.items()}

3. Categorical Features: One-Hot Encoding of (User, Bundle)¶

We treat the (user_id, bundle_id) pair as a purely categorical feature and one-hot encode it.

  • Each column in X_train_cat corresponds to a user or bundle category (depending on how OneHotEncoder is configured).
  • This captures ID-specific biases similar to standard one-hot logistic regression.
In [ ]:
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder

enc = OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='ignore')

X_all_pairs = np.vstack([X_train_pairs, X_test_pairs])
enc.fit(X_all_pairs)

X_train_cat = enc.transform(X_train_pairs)
X_test_cat  = enc.transform(X_test_pairs)

4. Numeric Features: Popularity, Activity, Bundle Size, Coverage¶

We also build a set of numeric features based on graph statistics. For each pair ((u, b)) we compute:

  1. Bundle popularity $\text{pop}(b)$ (defined above).
  2. User activity $\text{act}(u)$.
  3. Bundle size — number of individual games in bundle $b$:

$$ \text{size}(b) = |I(b)| $$

  1. Coverage — how much of the bundle the user already owns:

$$ \text{cov}(u, b) = \begin{cases} \frac{|I(u) \cap I(b)|}{|I(b)|}, & |I(b)| > 0 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

where $I(u)$ is the set of items owned by user $u$, and $I(b)$ is the set of items contained in bundle $b$.

All numeric features are standardized using StandardScaler:

$$ x' = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} $$

so each dimension has approximately zero mean and unit variance.

In [ ]:
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
def build_numeric_features(pairs):
    users = pairs[:, 0]
    bundles = pairs[:, 1]

    pop_list = []
    act_list = []
    size_list = []
    cov_list = []

    for u, b in zip(users, bundles):
        pop_list.append(bundle_popularity.get(int(b), 0.0))
        act_list.append(user_activity.get(int(u), 0.0))

        b_item = bundle_items_list.get(int(b), 0.0)
        size = len(b_item)

        u_item = user_item_map.get(int(u), 0.0)
        if size > 0:
            cov = len(u_item & b_item) / float(size)
        else:
            cov = 0
        size_list.append(size)
        cov_list.append(cov)

    return np.vstack([pop_list, act_list, size_list, cov_list]).T

num_train = build_numeric_features(X_train_pairs)
num_test  = build_numeric_features(X_test_pairs)

scaler = StandardScaler()
num_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(num_train)
num_test_scaled  = scaler.transform(num_test)

5. Final Feature Matrix — Why Graph-Sampling Matters¶

After building both the categorical (one-hot) and numeric features, we concatenate them into a single sparse feature matrix.

To inspect how informative the numeric features are, we compare the mean values of positives vs. negatives:

pop   mean pos/neg: 0.516 0.516
size  mean pos/neg: 0.048 0.048
cov   mean pos/neg: 0.219 0.217
1. Why are positive and negative distributions almost identical?¶

This behavior highlights an important property of graph-sampling, especially the cross-edge hard negative sampling used earlier:

✔ Hard negatives are sampled to closely resemble positive samples Because each negative bundle was chosen by crossing two real positive edges $(u1, b1)$ and $(u2, b2)$, the negative bundle:

  • is popular enough to appear in the graph,
  • occurs as a positive example for another user,
  • often has similar bundle size,
  • may contain overlapping items to the user's history.

Thus the negative samples are not random uninformed negatives, but hard negatives that share similar feature statistics with true positives.

Formally, for any negative sample $(u_1, b_2)$, we know that $b_2$ was a positive bundle for some user $u_2$:

$$ b_2 \in \mathrm{B}(u_2), \qquad b_2 \notin \mathrm{B}(u_1) $$

Because of this, distributions satisfy approximately:

$$ \mathbb{E}[\text{pop}(b) \mid y = 1] \approx \mathbb{E}[\text{pop}(b) \mid y = 0] $$

and similarly for size, coverage, etc.

✔ This is strong evidence that graph-sampling produces hard, realistic negatives

If we had sampled negatives randomly, we would see:

  • much lower popularity for negatives,
  • smaller bundle sizes,
  • close-to-zero coverage.

But with graph-sampling, the model faces difficult learning tasks where positives and negatives look nearly identical based on naive statistics.

2. Why This Also Shows Logistic Regression Is Not Suitable for This Task¶

The near-identical feature distributions between positive and negative samples also reveal a deeper issue:

this recommendation task is fundamentally not well-modeled by a linear classifier such as logistic regression.

Logistic regression makes a key assumption:

$$ \hat{p}(y = 1 \mid x) = \sigma(w^\top x + b) $$

That is, the model can only separate positive and negative examples through a linear boundary in the feature space.

However, in our setting:

  • Bundle popularity, size, and coverage do not differ meaningfully between positives and hard negatives.
  • User and bundle identities are one-hot encoded into extremely high-dimensional sparse vectors.
  • The relationship “user likes bundle” is highly non-linear, depending on latent compatibility patterns that cannot be expressed through linear weights.

Because of graph-sampling:

  • negatives are intentionally chosen to be very close to positives in feature space,
  • simple global statistics (popularity, activity, coverage...) have almost no predictive power,
  • linear models cannot capture the underlying structure of the user–bundle bipartite graph.

In effect, logistic regression ends up modeling:

  • global popularity biases,
  • user activity biases,

but not the meaningful user–bundle matching preferences that drive recommendation.

This mismatch explains why logistic regression yields:

  • low Recall@K,
  • weak ranking ability,
  • and overall performance far below MF, neural model, or BPR.
In [ ]:
from scipy import sparse
X_train = sparse.hstack([X_train_cat, num_train_scaled])
X_test  = sparse.hstack([X_test_cat,  num_test_scaled])

pos = num_train[y_train == 1]
neg = num_train[y_train == 0]

print("pop   mean pos/neg:", pos[:,0].mean(), neg[:,0].mean())
print("size  mean pos/neg:", pos[:,1].mean(), neg[:,1].mean())
print("cov   mean pos/neg:", pos[:,3].mean(), neg[:,3].mean())

pop   mean pos/neg: 0.5164968575385094 0.5164968575385094
size  mean pos/neg: 0.061077060468039644 0.061077060468039644
cov   mean pos/neg: 0.22154646262076658 0.21818172694852392

6. Logistic Regression Model (SGDClassifier)¶

We train a linear logistic regression model using stochastic gradient descent:

  • loss="log_loss": binary logistic loss

$$ \ell(y, \hat{p}) = -\left[y \log \hat{p} + (1 - y)\log(1 - \hat{p})\right] $$

  • The model learns a weight vector $w$ and bias $b$, predicting

$$ \hat{p}(y = 1 \mid x) = \sigma(w^\top x + b) $$

where $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the sigmoid function.

In [ ]:
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.linear_model import SGDClassifier
clf = SGDClassifier(loss="log_loss",penalty="l2",alpha=1e-5,max_iter=20,tol=1e-3)
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[ ]:
SGDClassifier(alpha=1e-05, loss='log_loss', max_iter=20)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
loss  'log_loss'
penalty  'l2'
alpha  1e-05
l1_ratio  0.15
fit_intercept  True
max_iter  20
tol  0.001
shuffle  True
verbose  0
epsilon  0.1
n_jobs  None
random_state  None
learning_rate  'optimal'
eta0  0.0
power_t  0.5
early_stopping  False
validation_fraction  0.1
n_iter_no_change  5
class_weight  None
warm_start  False
average  False

7. Evaluation: User-wise Recall@K for Logistic Regression¶

We evaluate ranking quality using a user-grouped Recall@K: For each user $u$:

  1. Collect all test pairs $(u, b)$ and their:

    • true labels $y \in \{0, 1\}$
    • predicted scores $p$

  2. Sort bundles for this user by $p$ in descending order.

  3. Take the top $k$ bundles and compute:

$$ \text{Recall@}k(u) = \frac{\#\{\text{positive bundles of } u \text{ in top } k\}} {\#\{\text{all positive bundles of } u\}} $$

  1. The final metric is the average over users with at least one positive:

$$ \text{Recall@}k = \frac{1}{|\mathrm{U}_+|} \sum_{u \in \mathrm{U}_+} \text{Recall@}k(u) $$

where $\mathrm{U}_+$ is the set of users with at least one positive in the test set.

In [ ]:
def recall_at_k_logistic(y_true, y_score, users_test, k):
    user2labels = {}
    user2scores = {}

    for u, y, p in zip(users_test, y_true, y_score):
        user2labels.setdefault(u, []).append(y)
        user2scores.setdefault(u, []).append(p)

    recalls = []
    for u in user2labels:
        labels = np.array(user2labels[u])
        scores = np.array(user2scores[u])

        if labels.sum() == 0:
            continue

        topk_idx = scores.argsort()[::-1][:k]

        hits = labels[topk_idx].sum()
        total_positives = labels.sum()

        recalls.append(hits / total_positives)

    if len(recalls) == 0:
        return 0.0
    return np.mean(recalls)


def precision_at_k_logistic(y_true, y_score, users_test, k):
    user2labels = {}
    user2scores = {}

    for u, y, p in zip(users_test, y_true, y_score):
        user2labels.setdefault(u, []).append(y)
        user2scores.setdefault(u, []).append(p)

    precisions = []
    for u in user2labels:
        labels = np.array(user2labels[u])
        scores = np.array(user2scores[u])

        topk_idx = scores.argsort()[::-1][:k]

        hits = labels[topk_idx].sum()

        precisions.append(hits / k)

    if len(precisions) == 0:
        return 0.0
    return np.mean(precisions)


def compute_all_metrics_logistic(y_true, y_score, users_test):
    ks = list(range(5, 55, 5))
    recalls = []
    precisions = []
    for k in ks:
        r = recall_at_k_logistic(y_true, y_score, users_test, k)
        p = precision_at_k_logistic(y_true, y_score, users_test, k)
        recalls.append(r)
        precisions.append(p)
    return ks, recalls, precisions


from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, roc_auc_score

y_pred = clf.predict(X_test)
y_pred_proba = clf.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

acc = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_pred_proba)

users_test = X_test_pairs[:, 0]

ks, recalls, precisions = compute_all_metrics_logistic(y_test, y_pred_proba, users_test)

recalls_a, precisions_a = recalls, precisions

print("Accuracy:", acc)
print("AUC:", auc)
for k, r, p in zip(ks, recalls, precisions):
    print(f"Precision@{k} = {p:.4f}  |  Recall@{k} = {r:.4f}")

plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
plt.plot(ks, precisions, marker="o", label='Precision@k', linewidth=2)
plt.plot(ks, recalls, marker="s", label='Recall@k', linewidth=2)
plt.xlabel("k", fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Score", fontsize=12)
plt.title("Logistic Regression Precision@k and Recall@k", fontsize=14)
plt.ylim(0, 1)
plt.legend(fontsize=11)
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Accuracy: 0.5205488359994798
AUC: 0.5019212699170967
Precision@5 = 0.2237  |  Recall@5 = 0.0333
Precision@10 = 0.2535  |  Recall@10 = 0.0755
Precision@15 = 0.2975  |  Recall@15 = 0.1345
Precision@20 = 0.3498  |  Recall@20 = 0.2143
Precision@25 = 0.4041  |  Recall@25 = 0.3135
Precision@30 = 0.4487  |  Recall@30 = 0.4174
Precision@35 = 0.4799  |  Recall@35 = 0.5157
Precision@40 = 0.4981  |  Recall@40 = 0.6025
Precision@45 = 0.5052  |  Recall@45 = 0.6742
Precision@50 = 0.5036  |  Recall@50 = 0.7317

(B) BPR¶

Here, we replicate Bayesian Personalized Ranking (BPR) from Pathak et al. (2017). BPR is trained in two stages. In the first stage, we train an item BPR to score items for users. For every user-item pair, BPR employs an item bias scalar, a $k$-dimension user vector and a $k$-dimension item vector to compute a score: $$\hat{x}_{u,i} = \beta_i + P_u \cdot Q_i$$ where $\beta_i$ is the item bias, $p_u$ and $q_i$ are $k$-dimensional latent parameter vectors for user $u$ and item $i$ respectively. We use stochastic gradient descent (SGD) to train the parameters on the $(u, i_p, i_n)$ triplets, with the goal to maximize: $$\sum_{(u, i_p, i_n) \in D} \log(\sigma(x_{u,i_p}(\theta) - x_{u,i_n}(\theta))) - \lambda ||\theta||^2$$ With the trained $\beta_i$, $p_u$ and $q_i$, we proceed to the second stage to train a bundle BPR. A bundle score is the average of individual item scores, adjusted with newly trained parameters: $k$-dimensional $\kappa$ on $\beta_i$, $k \times k$ adjustment matrices $\mu$ and $\omega$: $$\hat{x}_{u,b} = \frac{1}{|B_b|} \sum_{i \in B_b} \bigl[\kappa \beta_i + (\mu P_u)\cdot(\omega Q_i)\bigr]$$ They are optimized on the $(u, b_p, b_n)$ training triplet data with the same criterion: $$\sum_{(u, b_p, b_n) \in D} \log(\sigma(x_{u,b_p}(\theta) - x_{u,b_n}(\theta))) - \lambda ||\theta||^2$$

In [ ]:
# Recreate users_items_train properly
users_items_train = list(zip(item_sgd_users, sgd_pos_items, sgd_neg_items))

max_user_idx = max(u for u, _, _ in users_items_train) if users_items_train else 0
max_item_idx = max(max(i, j) for _, i, j in users_items_train) if users_items_train else 0

# Check the data dimensions
print(f"Number of users: {len(user_item_map_bpr)}")
print(f"Number of items: {len(items_set)}")
print(f"Max user index in users_items_train: {max_user_idx}")
print(f"Max item index in users_items_train: {max_item_idx}")
print(f"Number of training samples: {len(users_items_train)}")

Number of users: 71504
Number of items: 34239
Max user index in users_items_train: 88308
Max item index in users_items_train: 22465
Number of training samples: 5153210
In [ ]:
def item_bpr_sgd_model(users_items_train, num_users, num_items, k=10, max_iters=20, lr=0.01, reg=0.001):
    """
    BPR-based SGD for pairwise ranking optimization.

    Model: x_{u,i} = beta_i + u_factors · i_factors

    Objective: maximize sum log(sigmoid(x_{u,i_p} - x_{u,i_n})) - lambda ||theta||^2

    Parameters:
    - users_items_train: iterable of (u, i_pos, i_neg) tuples
    - k: latent dimension
    - lr: learning rate
    - reg: L2 regularization coefficient
    """
    rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

    # Initialize parameters
    user_factors = rng.normal(0, 0.01, size=(num_users, k))
    item_factors = rng.normal(0, 0.01, size=(num_items, k))
    beta_i = np.zeros(num_items, dtype=np.float64)

    # Convert to list if needed for multiple epochs
    train_list = list(users_items_train)
    n_train = len(train_list)

    for iteration in range(1, max_iters + 1):
        # Shuffle training data each epoch
        indices = rng.permutation(n_train)

        total_loss = 0.0
        for idx in indices:
            u_idx, i_pos_idx, i_neg_idx = train_list[idx]

            # Compute predictions for positive and negative items
            x_pos = beta_i[i_pos_idx] + np.dot(user_factors[u_idx], item_factors[i_pos_idx])
            x_neg = beta_i[i_neg_idx] + np.dot(user_factors[u_idx], item_factors[i_neg_idx])

            # Compute x_uij = x_{u,i_pos} - x_{u,i_neg}
            x_uij = x_pos - x_neg

            # Compute log(sigmoid(x_uij)) with numerical stability
            # log(sigmoid(x)) = -log(1 + exp(-x))
            # For stability: use -softplus(-x) where softplus(x) = log(1 + exp(x))
            if x_uij >= 0:
                loss_contribution = -np.log(1.0 + np.exp(-x_uij))
            else:
                # log(sigmoid(x)) = x - log(1 + exp(x)) when x < 0
                loss_contribution = x_uij - np.log(1.0 + np.exp(x_uij))

            # Gradient: d/dx_uij[log(sigmoid(x_uij))] = sigmoid(-x_uij) = 1 - sigmoid(x_uij)
            sigmoid_x = 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-np.clip(x_uij, -100, 100)))  # Clip for stability

            total_loss += loss_contribution

            # Gradient of log(sigmoid(x_uij)) w.r.t x_uij is: 1 - sigmoid(x_uij)
            # This is the gradient multiplier for maximization
            gradient_multiplier = 1.0 - sigmoid_x

            # Update biases
            # d/d(beta_i_pos) = gradient_multiplier (since x_uij increases with beta_i_pos)
            # d/d(beta_i_neg) = -gradient_multiplier (since x_uij decreases with beta_i_neg)
            beta_i[i_pos_idx] += lr * (gradient_multiplier - reg * beta_i[i_pos_idx])
            beta_i[i_neg_idx] += lr * (-gradient_multiplier - reg * beta_i[i_neg_idx])

            # Update latent factors
            # d/d(p_u) = gradient_multiplier * (q_i_pos - q_i_neg)
            # d/d(q_i_pos) = gradient_multiplier * p_u
            # d/d(q_i_neg) = -gradient_multiplier * p_u
            p_u = user_factors[u_idx].copy()
            q_pos = item_factors[i_pos_idx].copy()
            q_neg = item_factors[i_neg_idx].copy()

            user_factors[u_idx] += lr * (gradient_multiplier * (q_pos - q_neg) - reg * p_u)
            item_factors[i_pos_idx] += lr * (gradient_multiplier * p_u - reg * q_pos)
            item_factors[i_neg_idx] += lr * (-gradient_multiplier * p_u - reg * q_neg)

        # Compute regularization loss
        reg_loss = reg * (np.sum(user_factors**2) + np.sum(item_factors**2) + np.sum(beta_i**2))
        total_loss -= reg_loss

        if iteration % 1 == 0:
            print(f"BPR-SGD Iteration {iteration}")
            print(f"  Total Loss (approx) = {total_loss:.6f}")
            print(f"  Avg Loss per sample = {total_loss / n_train:.6f}")

    return beta_i, user_factors, item_factors
In [ ]:
# Train BPR model on items
# Use the actual max indices from the data, not the count of non-empty entries
print(f"Training BPR with:")
print(f"  num_users = {max_user_idx + 1} (max user idx: {max_user_idx})")
print(f"  num_items = {max_item_idx + 1} (max item idx: {max_item_idx})")

beta_i, user_factors, item_factors = item_bpr_sgd_model(
    users_items_train,
    num_users=max_user_idx + 1,
    num_items=max_item_idx + 1,
    k=20,
    max_iters=20,
    lr=0.1,
    reg=0.001
)

Training BPR with:
  num_users = 88309 (max user idx: 88308)
  num_items = 22466 (max item idx: 22465)
BPR-SGD Iteration 1
  Total Loss (approx) = -3113138.433507

In [ ]:
def bundle_bpr_sgd_model(users_bundles_train, bundle_item_map, beta_i, user_factors, item_factors,
                         num_users, num_bundles, k=10, max_iters=10, lr=0.01, reg=0.001):
    """
    Bundle BPR-SGD using pre-trained item embeddings.

    Model: x_{u,b} = (1/|B_b|) * sum_{i in B_b} [kappa · beta_i + (mu · p_u) · (omega · q_i)]

    Where:
    - kappa: k-dimensional weight vector for item biases
    - mu: k * k transformation matrix for user factors
    - omega: k * k transformation matrix for item factors

    Parameters:
    - users_bundles_train: list of (user_idx, bundle_pos_idx, bundle_neg_idx) tuples
    - bundle_item_map: dict mapping bundle_idx -> set of item indices
    - beta_i, user_factors, item_factors: pre-trained from item BPR
    - k: latent dimension (must match item_factors dimension)
    - max_iters, lr, reg: training hyperparameters
    """
    rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

    # Initialize transformation parameters
    kappa = rng.normal(0, 0.01, size=k)
    mu = rng.normal(0, 0.01, size=(k, k))
    omega = rng.normal(0, 0.01, size=(k, k))

    train_list = list(users_bundles_train)
    n_train = len(train_list)

    def predict_bundle_score(u_idx, b_idx):
        """Compute bundle score for user u and bundle b."""
        items_in_bundle = bundle_item_map.get(b_idx, set())
        if not items_in_bundle:
            return 0.0

        p_u = user_factors[u_idx]  # k-dimensional
        mu_pu = np.dot(mu, p_u)    # k-dimensional: mu · p_u

        total_score = 0.0
        for i_idx in items_in_bundle:
            q_i = item_factors[i_idx]          # k-dimensional
            omega_qi = np.dot(omega, q_i)      # k-dimensional: omega · q_i

            # Item contribution: kappa · beta_i + (mu · p_u) · (omega · q_i)
            item_score = np.dot(kappa, beta_i[i_idx] * np.ones(k)) + np.dot(mu_pu, omega_qi)
            total_score += item_score

        # Average over bundle items
        return total_score / len(items_in_bundle)

    for iteration in range(1, max_iters + 1):
        indices = rng.permutation(n_train)
        total_loss = 0.0

        for idx in indices:
            u_idx, b_pos_idx, b_neg_idx = train_list[idx]

            # Compute bundle scores
            x_pos = predict_bundle_score(u_idx, b_pos_idx)
            x_neg = predict_bundle_score(u_idx, b_neg_idx)
            x_uij = x_pos - x_neg

            # Compute loss contribution (numerically stable)
            if x_uij >= 0:
                loss_contribution = -np.log(1.0 + np.exp(-x_uij))
            else:
                loss_contribution = x_uij - np.log(1.0 + np.exp(x_uij))

            total_loss += loss_contribution

            # Gradient multiplier
            sigmoid_x = 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-np.clip(x_uij, -100, 100)))
            grad_mult = 1.0 - sigmoid_x

            # Get bundle items
            items_pos = bundle_item_map.get(b_pos_idx, set())
            items_neg = bundle_item_map.get(b_neg_idx, set())

            if not items_pos or not items_neg:
                continue

            n_pos = len(items_pos)
            n_neg = len(items_neg)

            # Pre-compute user transformations
            p_u = user_factors[u_idx]
            mu_pu = np.dot(mu, p_u)

            # Initialize gradients
            grad_kappa = np.zeros(k)
            grad_mu = np.zeros((k, k))
            grad_omega = np.zeros((k, k))

            # Gradient from positive bundle (increase score)
            for i_idx in items_pos:
                q_i = item_factors[i_idx]
                omega_qi = np.dot(omega, q_i)

                # d/d(kappa): beta_i / |B_pos|
                grad_kappa += (grad_mult / n_pos) * beta_i[i_idx] * np.ones(k)

                # d/d(mu): outer product of result with p_u, scaled by omega_qi
                # (mu·p_u)·(omega·q_i) differentiates to: (omega·q_i) ⊗ p_u
                grad_mu += (grad_mult / n_pos) * np.outer(omega_qi, p_u)

                # d/d(omega): outer product with q_i, scaled by mu_pu
                # (mu·p_u)·(omega·q_i) differentiates to: (mu·p_u) ⊗ q_i
                grad_omega += (grad_mult / n_pos) * np.outer(mu_pu, q_i)

            # Gradient from negative bundle (decrease score)
            for i_idx in items_neg:
                q_i = item_factors[i_idx]
                omega_qi = np.dot(omega, q_i)

                grad_kappa -= (grad_mult / n_neg) * beta_i[i_idx] * np.ones(k)
                grad_mu -= (grad_mult / n_neg) * np.outer(omega_qi, p_u)
                grad_omega -= (grad_mult / n_neg) * np.outer(mu_pu, q_i)

            # Update parameters with regularization
            kappa += lr * (grad_kappa - reg * kappa)
            mu += lr * (grad_mu - reg * mu)
            omega += lr * (grad_omega - reg * omega)

        # Compute regularization loss
        reg_loss = reg * (np.sum(kappa**2) + np.sum(mu**2) + np.sum(omega**2))
        total_loss -= reg_loss

        if iteration % 1 == 0:
            print(f"Bundle BPR-SGD Iteration {iteration}")
            print(f"  Total Loss = {total_loss:.6f}")
            print(f"  Avg Loss per sample = {total_loss / n_train:.6f}")

    return kappa, mu, omega
In [ ]:
# Create bundle training data from graph_sampling output
sgd_users, sgd_pos_bundles, sgd_neg_bundles = graph_sampling(
    len(training_data_bpr)*30, training_data_bpr, user_bundle_map_train_bpr
)
users_bundles_train = list(zip(sgd_users, sgd_pos_bundles, sgd_neg_bundles))

print(f"Training Bundle BPR with:")
print(f"  Number of users = {len(user_factors)}")
print(f"  Number of bundles: {len(bundle_item_map)}")
print(f"  Number of training samples: {len(users_bundles_train)}")
print(f"  Latent dimension k: {user_factors.shape[1]}")

# Train bundle BPR model
kappa, mu, omega = bundle_bpr_sgd_model(
    users_bundles_train,
    bundle_item_map,
    beta_i,
    user_factors,
    item_factors,
    num_users=len(user_factors),
    num_bundles=len(bundle_item_map),
    k=user_factors.shape[1],  # Use same k as item model
    max_iters=10,
    lr=0.005,
    reg=0.001
)

print("\nBundle model training complete!")
print(f"Learned parameters: kappa shape={kappa.shape}, mu shape={mu.shape}, omega shape={omega.shape}")

In [ ]:
def predict_bundle_score(u_idx, b_idx, kappa, mu, omega, beta_i, user_factors, item_factors, bundle_item_map):
    """
    Compute bundle score for user u and bundle b using learned transformation parameters.
    """
    items_in_bundle = bundle_item_map.get(b_idx, set())
    if not items_in_bundle:
        return 0.0

    p_u = user_factors[u_idx]  # k-dimensional
    mu_pu = np.dot(mu, p_u)    # k-dimensional: mu · p_u

    total_score = 0.0
    for i_idx in items_in_bundle:
        q_i = item_factors[i_idx]          # k-dimensional
        omega_qi = np.dot(omega, q_i)      # k-dimensional: omega · q_i

        # Item contribution: kappa · beta_i + (mu · p_u) · (omega · q_i)
        k = len(kappa)
        item_score = np.dot(kappa, beta_i[i_idx] * np.ones(k)) + np.dot(mu_pu, omega_qi)
        total_score += item_score

    # Average over bundle items
    return total_score / len(items_in_bundle)
In [ ]:
sgd_users_test_bpr, sgd_pos_bundles_test_bpr, sgd_neg_bundles_test_bpr = graph_sampling(
    len(test_data_bpr)*30, test_data_bpr, user_bundle_map_bpr
)

X_test_pairs_bpr = []
y_test_bpr = []

for u, b_pos, b_neg in zip(sgd_users_test_bpr, sgd_pos_bundles_test_bpr, sgd_neg_bundles_test_bpr):
    X_test_pairs_bpr.append([u, b_pos])
    y_test_bpr.append(1)
    X_test_pairs_bpr.append([u, b_neg])
    y_test_bpr.append(0)

print(f"Total test samples for BPR evaluation: {len(X_test_pairs_bpr)}")

0
100000
200000
300000
Total test samples for BPR evaluation: 1230240
In [ ]:
# Evaluate Bundle BPR Model using predict_bundle_score
print("="*60)
print("Evaluating Bundle BPR Model on Test Data")
print("="*60)

# Use X_test_pairs_bpr and y_test_bpr from cell 38
print(f"Total test samples: {len(X_test_pairs_bpr)}")

# Compute scores for all test pairs
y_pred_scores = []
for user_idx, bundle_idx in X_test_pairs_bpr:
    score = predict_bundle_score(
        user_idx,
        bundle_idx,
        kappa,
        mu,
        omega,
        beta_i,
        user_factors,
        item_factors,
        bundle_item_map_bpr
    )
    y_pred_scores.append(score)

y_pred_scores = np.array(y_pred_scores)
y_test_bpr = np.array(y_test_bpr)

from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score, accuracy_score

auc = roc_auc_score(y_test_bpr, y_pred_scores)
print(f"\nBundle BPR AUC: {auc:.4f}")

y_pred_binary = []
i = 0
for i in range(len(y_pred_scores)):
    if i % 2 == 1:
        if y_pred_scores[i - 1] > y_pred_scores[i]:
            y_pred_binary.append(1)
            y_pred_binary.append(0)
        else:
            y_pred_binary.append(0)
            y_pred_binary.append(1)

accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test_bpr, y_pred_binary)
print(f"Bundle BPR Accuracy: {accuracy:.4f}")

# Calculate Recall@K and Precision@K metrics
# Group test data by user
user_bundle_scores = defaultdict(list)  # user -> [(bundle, score, label)]
for (user_idx, bundle_idx), score, label in zip(X_test_pairs_bpr, y_pred_scores, y_test_bpr):
    user_bundle_scores[user_idx].append((bundle_idx, score, label))

# Compute Recall@K and Precision@K for each user
recall_metrics = {}
precision_metrics = {}
for k in range(5, 55, 5):
    recalls = []
    precisions = []
    for user_idx, bundle_data in user_bundle_scores.items():
        # Sort by score (descending)
        sorted_bundles = sorted(bundle_data, key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

        # Get top-K bundles
        top_k = sorted_bundles[:k]

        # Count positives in top-K
        positive_in_topk = sum(1 for _, _, label in top_k if label == 1)

        # Count total positives for this user
        total_positives = sum(1 for _, _, label in bundle_data if label == 1)

        # Calculate Precision@K: positive_in_topk / k
        precision = positive_in_topk / k
        precisions.append(precision)

        # Calculate Recall@K: positive_in_topk / total_positives
        recall = positive_in_topk / total_positives
        recalls.append(recall)

    precision_metrics[f'Precision@{k}'] = np.mean(precisions) if precisions else 0.0
    recall_metrics[f'Recall@{k}'] = np.mean(recalls) if recalls else 0.0
    print(f"  Precision@{k}: {precision_metrics[f'Precision@{k}']:.4f}  |  Recall@{k}: {recall_metrics[f'Recall@{k}']:.4f}")

print("="*60)

In [ ]:
ks = list(range(5, 55, 5))

precisions = [precision_metrics[rk] for rk in precision_metrics]
recalls = [recall_metrics[rk] for rk in recall_metrics]

precisions_b, recalls_b = precisions, recalls

plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
plt.plot(ks, precisions, marker='o', label='Precision@k', linewidth=2)
plt.plot(ks, recalls, marker='s', label='Recall@k', linewidth=2)
plt.xlabel("k", fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Precision/Recall Score", fontsize=12)
plt.title("BPR Precision@k and Recall@k", fontsize=14)
plt.ylim(0, 1)
plt.legend(fontsize=10)
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

(C) Latent Factor Model¶

Dataset Wrapper¶

We wrap $(u,b,label)$ triples into a simple PyTorch Dataset, which enables efficient mini-batch training using Dataloader.

In [ ]:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, roc_auc_score
In [ ]:
class PairDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, pairs, labels):
        self.users = torch.LongTensor(pairs[:, 0])
        self.bundles = torch.LongTensor(pairs[:, 1])
        self.labels = torch.FloatTensor(labels)

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.labels)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        return self.users[idx], self.bundles[idx], self.labels[idx]


class MF(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_users, num_bundles, emb_dim=32):
        super().__init__()
        self.user_emb = nn.Embedding(num_users, emb_dim)
        self.bundle_emb = nn.Embedding(num_bundles, emb_dim)

        self.user_bias = nn.Embedding(num_users, 1)
        self.bundle_bias = nn.Embedding(num_bundles, 1)
        self.global_bias = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1))

        nn.init.normal_(self.user_emb.weight, std=0.01)
        nn.init.normal_(self.bundle_emb.weight, std=0.01)
        nn.init.zeros_(self.user_bias.weight)
        nn.init.zeros_(self.bundle_bias.weight)

    def forward(self, user_ids, bundle_ids):
        u = self.user_emb(user_ids)
        b = self.bundle_emb(bundle_ids)
        dot = (u * b).sum(dim=1, keepdim=True)
        u_bias = self.user_bias(user_ids)
        b_bias = self.bundle_bias(bundle_ids)

        logits = dot + u_bias + b_bias + self.global_bias
        return logits.squeeze(1)

1. Latent Representation¶

For each user $u$ and bundle $b$:

  • User embedding: $$ p_u \in \mathbb{R}^d $$

  • Bundle embedding: $$ q_b \in \mathbb{R}^d $$

The dimensionality $d$ controls model capacity.

2. MF Scoring Function¶

The predicted score is the inner product of latent factors:

$$ \hat{y}_{u,b} = p_u^\top q_b $$

This models the similarity of user preferences and bundle characteristics. But the full version additionally includes bias terms for better calibration.

With Bias Terms¶

We include:

  • User bias: $b_u$
  • Bundle bias: $b_b$
  • Global bias: $b_0$

Thus the full scoring function becomes:

$$ s(u,b) = p_u^\top q_b + b_u + b_b + b_0 $$

This structure captures:

  • global attractiveness of bundles (e.g., very popular ones),
  • user activeness (some users interact with many bundles),
  • but keeps personal preference in the latent vectors (p_u, q_b).
In [ ]:
emb_dim    = 1
batch_size = 2048
lr         = 5e-3
n_epochs   = 6
device     = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")

train_dataset = PairDataset(X_train_pairs, y_train)
test_dataset  = PairDataset(X_test_pairs, y_test)

train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True)
test_loader  = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False)

model = MF(num_users=num_users, num_bundles=num_bundles, emb_dim=emb_dim).to(device)
criterion = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()
optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr)

3. Logistic Output Layer¶

For binary interaction prediction (positive/negative), we apply the sigmoid function to convert raw scores into probabilities:

$$ \hat{p}_{u,b} = \sigma(s(u,b)) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-s(u,b)}} $$

This allows the use of binary cross-entropy loss (BCE).

4. Training Objective (Binary Cross-Entropy)¶

For each user–bundle pair $(u, b)$ with label $y_{u,b} \in \{0,1\}$:

$$ \mathrm{L} = -\left[y_{u,b} \log \hat{p}_{u,b} + (1 - y_{u,b}) \log(1 - \hat{p}_{u,b})\right] $$

MF parameters $\theta = \{p_u, q_b, b_u, b_b, b_0\}$ are updated by minimizing total loss:

$$ \mathrm{J}(\theta) = \sum_{(u,b)} \mathrm{L}_{u,b} $$

In PyTorch:

criterion = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()

This automatically combines sigmoid + BCE.

5. Forward Pass (Detailed Formula Breakdown)¶

Explanation by Formula¶
  1. Latent vectors:

$$ u = p_u,\qquad b = q_b $$

  1. Inner product:

$$ \text{dot}(u,b) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} p_{u,i} q_{b,i} $$

  1. Add biases:

$$ \text{logit}(u,b) = p_u^\top q_b + b_u + b_b + b_0 $$

  1. Returned logits go into BCE for probability estimation.

6. Training Loop (Detailed Explanation)¶

Gradient Update¶

$$ \theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \, \frac{\partial \mathrm{J}}{\partial \theta} $$

7. Why MF Fits This Task Better¶

✔ Learns latent structure from graph-sampled hard negatives¶

Graph sampling produces:

  • positives from true interactions
  • negatives drawn from other users’ positives, forming strong hard negatives

These negatives often have:

  • similar popularity
  • similar bundle size
  • similar item coverage

MF is well-equipped to distinguish such subtle differences by learning latent user–bundle compatibility through:

$$p_u^T q_b$$

Where logistic regression collapses to popularity statistics, MF captures deeper preference structure.

✔ Embeddings generalize to unseen user–bundle combinations¶

MF naturally generalizes to pairs never seen during training.

If both embeddings $p_u$ and $q_b$ were shaped by other interactions, MF can still estimate, which allows MF to handle sparse or unseen combinations without requiring explicit side features.

✔ Clean, efficient, and easy to extend¶

MF offers a clean and efficient solution to the recommendation problem.

The entire model consists of two embedding tables and a dot product:

$$\text{score}(u, b) = p_u^T q_b + \text{biases}$$

This simplicity gives MF several meta-advantages:

  • fast training and inference
  • minimal memory footprint
  • low engineering overhead
  • easy extension to advanced models such as NeuMF, LightGCN, or BPR-MF

8. Limitations of MF¶

Although MF works well for this dataset, several important limitations remain:

❗MF optimizes classification, not ranking¶

In this implementation MF uses BCE loss, which trains the model as a binary classifier. This means MF does not explicitly encourage positive bundles to rank above negatives. As a result, MF may underperform on ranking metrics (like Recall@K) compared to methods such as BPR that directly optimize relative ordering.

❗Dot-product restricts the model expressive ability¶

MF models compatibility using a simple linear interaction:

$$s(u, b) = p_u^\top q_b$$

This imposes a linear compatibility function between user and bundle. Consequently, MF cannot capture:

  • complex feature interactions
  • higher-order patterns
  • non-linear user preferences

This expressiveness limitation makes MF less flexible than neural recommenders such as NeuMF or DeepFM.

In [ ]:
def recall_at_k_logistic(y_true, y_score, users_test, k):
    user2labels = {}
    user2scores = {}

    for u, y, p in zip(users_test, y_true, y_score):
        user2labels.setdefault(u, []).append(y)
        user2scores.setdefault(u, []).append(p)

    recalls = []
    for u in user2labels:
        labels = np.array(user2labels[u])
        scores = np.array(user2scores[u])

        if labels.sum() == 0:
            continue

        topk_idx = scores.argsort()[::-1][:k]

        hits = labels[topk_idx].sum()
        total_positives = labels.sum()

        recalls.append(hits / total_positives)
    if len(recalls) == 0:
        return 0.0
    return np.mean(recalls)


def precision_at_k_logistic(y_true, y_score, users_test, k):
    user2labels = {}
    user2scores = {}

    for u, y, p in zip(users_test, y_true, y_score):
        user2labels.setdefault(u, []).append(y)
        user2scores.setdefault(u, []).append(p)

    precisions = []
    for u in user2labels:
        labels = np.array(user2labels[u])
        scores = np.array(user2scores[u])

        topk_idx = scores.argsort()[::-1][:k]

        hits = labels[topk_idx].sum()

        precisions.append(hits / k)

    if len(precisions) == 0:
        return 0.0
    return np.mean(precisions)
In [ ]:
def recall_at_k(all_labels, all_probs, all_users, k):
    user2labels = {}
    user2scores = {}

    for u, y, p in zip(all_users, all_labels, all_probs):
        user2labels.setdefault(u, []).append(y)
        user2scores.setdefault(u, []).append(p)

    recalls = []
    for u in user2labels:
        labels = np.array(user2labels[u])
        scores = np.array(user2scores[u])

        if labels.sum() == 0:
            continue

        topk_idx = scores.argsort()[::-1][:k]

        hits = labels[topk_idx].sum()
        total_positives = labels.sum()

        recalls.append(hits / total_positives)

    return np.mean(recalls)

def precision_at_k(all_labels, all_probs, all_users, k):
    user2labels = {}
    user2scores = {}

    for u, y, p in zip(all_users, all_labels, all_probs):
        user2labels.setdefault(u, []).append(y)
        user2scores.setdefault(u, []).append(p)

    precisions = []
    for u in user2labels:
        labels = np.array(user2labels[u])
        scores = np.array(user2scores[u])

        topk_idx = scores.argsort()[::-1][:k]

        hits = labels[topk_idx].sum()

        precisions.append(hits / k)

    return np.mean(precisions)

def evaluate(loader):
    model.eval()
    all_labels = []
    all_probs  = []
    all_users  = []

    with torch.no_grad():
        for users, bundles, labels in loader:
            users   = users.to(device)
            bundles = bundles.to(device)
            labels  = labels.to(device)

            logits = model(users, bundles)
            probs  = torch.sigmoid(logits)

            all_labels.append(labels.cpu())
            all_probs.append(probs.cpu())
            all_users.append(users.cpu())

    all_labels = torch.cat(all_labels).numpy()
    all_probs  = torch.cat(all_probs).numpy()
    all_users  = torch.cat(all_users).numpy()

    preds = (all_probs >= 0.5).astype("int32")

    acc = accuracy_score(all_labels, preds)
    auc = roc_auc_score(all_labels, all_probs)

    recalls = [
        recall_at_k(all_labels, all_probs, all_users, k)
        for k in range(5, 55, 5)
    ]

    precisions = [
        precision_at_k(all_labels, all_probs, all_users, k)
        for k in range(5, 55, 5)
    ]

    return acc, auc, recalls, precisions
In [ ]:
best_auc = 0
best_recalls = None
best_precisions = None

for epoch in range(1, n_epochs + 1):
    model.train()
    total_loss = 0.0

    for users, bundles, labels in train_loader:
        users   = users.to(device)
        bundles = bundles.to(device)
        labels  = labels.to(device)

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        logits = model(users, bundles)
        loss   = criterion(logits, labels)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

        total_loss += loss.item() * labels.size(0)

    avg_loss = total_loss / len(train_dataset)

    train_acc, train_auc, train_recalls, train_precisions = evaluate(train_loader)
    test_acc,  test_auc,  test_recalls, test_precisions  = evaluate(test_loader)

    print(f"\nEpoch {epoch:02d}")
    print(f"  Loss={avg_loss:.4f}")
    print(f"  Train: acc={train_acc:.4f}, auc={train_auc:.4f}, recalls={train_recalls}, precisions={train_precisions}")
    print(f"  Test : acc={test_acc:.4f},  auc={test_auc:.4f},  recalls={test_recalls}, precisions={test_precisions}")

    # Track best model by AUC
    if test_auc > best_auc:
        best_auc = test_auc
        best_recalls = test_recalls
        best_precisions = test_precisions
        torch.save(model.state_dict(), "best_model.pt")
        print("  --> Best model updated!")

# Load best model
model.load_state_dict(torch.load("best_model.pt"))

# Evaluate again
acc, auc, best_recalls, best_precisions = evaluate(test_loader)
ks = list(range(5, 55, 5))

precisions_c, recalls_c = best_precisions, best_recalls

print("Best Model Accuracy:", acc)
print("Best Model AUC:", auc)

print("\nBest Model Recalls:")
for k, r in zip(ks, best_recalls):
    print(f"  Recall@{k}: {r:.4f}")

print("\nBest Model Precisions:")
for k, r in zip(ks, best_precisions):
    print(f"  Precision@{k}: {r:.4f}")

plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
plt.plot(ks, best_precisions, marker="o", label='Precision@k', linewidth=2)
plt.plot(ks, best_recalls, marker="s", label='Recall@k', linewidth=2)
plt.xlabel("k", fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Score", fontsize=12)
plt.title("Latent Factor Precision@k and Recall@k", fontsize=14)
plt.ylim(0, 1)
plt.legend(fontsize=11)
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
